title: The Wizard Of Evergreen Terrace

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Homer crashes his car in opening credits
jokes.punchline: His signature frustrated exclamation
jokes.explanation: Iconic character catchphrase that perfectly encapsulates Homer's frustration. High character integration and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Bill and Marty's 5:00 news 'flush' with toilet sound effects
jokes.setup: Radio DJs presenting news segment
jokes.punchline: News 'flush' with literal toilet flushing sounds
jokes.explanation: Simple pun with sound effect gag. Functional but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Bill
dialogue.line: We interrupt this program for a news update.

dialogue.speaker: Marty
dialogue.line: That's right, Bill. And boy, is this news flush with...

dialogue.speaker: Bill
dialogue.line: Oh no, here it comes...

dialogue.speaker: Marty
dialogue.line: ...developments!

dialogue.speaker: Bill
dialogue.line: FLUSHHHHH

jokes.text: President Clinton's website URL innuendo
jokes.setup: DJs about to reveal Clinton's website URL
jokes.punchline: Implied dirty website address based on Clinton's reputation
jokes.explanation: Period-specific political humor that relies on Clinton scandal references. Low-effort topical joke.

dialogue.speaker: Bill
dialogue.line: Well, here it is, America. The Clinton website.

dialogue.speaker: Marty
dialogue.line: www.clinton.cum

jokes.text: 76.2? But I'm already 38.1. I've wasted half my life.
jokes.setup: Radio announces average male life expectancy is 76.2 years
jokes.punchline: Homer's overly precise calculation and dramatic reaction
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's tendency to overthink simple things in ridiculous ways. Sets up episode premise effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: 76.2? That means I only have... *counts on fingers* ...carry the one... 8 more years!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: *gasps in horror* EIGHT YEARS?! I haven't even learned to make a decent waffle yet!

jokes.text: Sir, do you need a tow truck? What are you talking about, Marge? I don't need a- [crash] Okay, send a truck.
jokes.setup: Homer denies needing help while talking to supposed Marge
jokes.punchline: Realizes he's talking to tow truck driver as he crashes
jokes.explanation: Well-structured misdirection joke with good timing. Homer's confusion and quick reversal work well.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I don't need any help, Marge. I'm fine!

dialogue.speaker: Tow Truck Driver
dialogue.line: I'm not Marge, buddy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wha—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Wouldn't you rather have your sugar bag? No, I don't deserve sugar.
jokes.setup: Marge offers Homer his sugar bag
jokes.punchline: Homer's dramatic self-denial over perceived life failure
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - connecting existential crisis to sugar consumption. Absurd but character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, here's your sugar bag.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, Marge. I've failed at everything. I'm a bad father, a bad husband, a bad worker. I don't deserve sugar.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's not true.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yes it is! Take it away before I change my mind!

jokes.text: No, Homer wasn't a great man... nor even an adequate man, and he certainly never accomplished anything.
jokes.setup: Homer imagining his funeral eulogy
jokes.punchline: Escalating brutally honest assessment
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that builds through escalation. Brutally honest in a way that's funny rather than just mean.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Homer was the richest man in the room... in terms of mass.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: He had a wife and three children, though I suspect the youngest may not be his.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Homer was a man of many talents. Not good talents, but many.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: He will not be missed.

jokes.text: Uh, President Lenny, you have anything to say? Nah. All right. Fair enough.
jokes.setup: Funeral scene continues
jokes.punchline: Lenny as president gives dismissive response
jokes.explanation: Absurd premise (President Lenny) with perfectly in-character dismissive response. Economical and fits character.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I'm president now, so... *waves hand dismissively* ...whatever.

jokes.text: There goes a real sack of crap. Indubitably, old chum.
jokes.setup: Continuing funeral fantasy
jokes.punchline: Crude assessment followed by pompous agreement
jokes.explanation: Contrast between vulgar and pretentious language creates humor. Good character voices.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: He's dead. That means I can finally sleep with his wife.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Barney, that's crude and inappropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: You're right. I apologize.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: However, I too have considered such an arrangement.

jokes.text: Promise me you won't vote for Lenny. Okay, but you've accomplished a lot.
jokes.setup: Homer wakes from nightmare
jokes.punchline: Still worried about President Lenny from his fantasy
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows Homer's confused state. Marge's response grounds the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge? Marge, wake up!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: What is it, Homer?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I just had the worst nightmare. Lenny was President!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: It was just a dream, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know, but what if it happens? What if Lenny actually becomes President?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that will never happen.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You don't know that, Marge. You don't know that.

jokes.text: You know how many memories I have? Three! Standing in line for a movie... having a key made and sitting here talking to you.
jokes.setup: Homer lamenting his lack of memories
jokes.punchline: Hilariously mundane list of 'memorable' moments
jokes.explanation: Brilliant specificity in the mundane memories. The banality makes it funnier than generic complaints would.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, I've had so many wonderful memories with my family. Let me think... there was the time I ate a whole box of donuts in one sitting. And then there was another time I ate a whole box of donuts in one sitting. Oh! And I can't forget when I fell asleep during Bart's school play. Wait, that happened twice too.

jokes.text: You're 39. [Homer yelps]
jokes.setup: Homer's dramatic speech about wasting half his life
jokes.punchline: Marge's matter-of-fact correction makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic timing and economy. Marge's practical nature deflates Homer's drama while making it worse.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I've wasted half my life!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you're only 36.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I KNOW!

jokes.text: Oh. I see you're having a party. I'll come back later.
jokes.setup: Family surprises Homer with celebration of his life
jokes.punchline: Homer completely misses that the party is for him
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer obliviousness. Perfect character moment that shows his self-absorption even in touching moments.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer! We have a surprise for you!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A surprise? For me? What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It's a party, Dad! A celebration of your life!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My life? Wow! Is there cake?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, this whole party is about you. Your achievements, your memories...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: About me? So who's the party actually for then?

jokes.text: Maybe I can pinpoint where my life went wrong.
jokes.setup: Family wants to show Homer his accomplishments
jokes.punchline: Homer sees it as opportunity to find his failures
jokes.explanation: Homer's pessimistic twist on family's well-meaning gesture. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, we've compiled all of your greatest accomplishments to show you how proud we are.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My failures? Finally! I've been looking everywhere for those!

jokes.text: Ah. All we did was grow some space tomatoes and sabotage Mir.
jokes.setup: Lisa says going to space is impressive
jokes.punchline: Homer casually mentions sabotaging Russian space station
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery of absurd accomplishment. The casual mention of international sabotage is brilliantly understated.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Going to space is impressive.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I sabotaged a Russian space station once.

jokes.text: Oh, I should've punted. Turn it off. Turn it off.
jokes.setup: Homer sees football footage of himself
jokes.punchline: Sports commentary on his own parenting
jokes.explanation: Clever use of sports metaphor for parenting moment. Good callback structure.

dialogue.speaker: Sports Commentator
dialogue.line: And there's Homer Simpson, employing what we in the business call the 'neglectful parent' defense.

dialogue.speaker: Sports Commentator
dialogue.line: A bold strategy — ignoring his children while eating donuts.

dialogue.speaker: Sports Commentator
dialogue.line: Let's see if it pays off for him.

jokes.text: KITT's voice slowing down and dying mid-sentence
jokes.setup: Fancy video message from KITT
jokes.punchline: Technology failure ruins the expensive gesture
jokes.explanation: Good visual/audio gag that subverts the expensive celebrity cameo. Technology failure is relatable.

jokes.text: You're a liar, honey- a dirty, rotten liar. Finish her! Finish her!
jokes.setup: Homer disputes Lisa's Edison facts
jokes.punchline: Fighting game references for family argument
jokes.explanation: Mortal Kombat reference applied to family discussion. Good escalation from doubt to combat metaphor.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, your facts about Thomas Edison are wrong!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, I have sources. Edison didn't actually invent the light bulb alone.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well I'm gonna use my special move on you!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa, is Dad doing hadoukens?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hadoken! Sonic boom! Spinning piledriver!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, please, we're trying to have a civil discussion.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Civil discussion? More like... MORTAL KOMBAT!

jokes.text: It's true. I read it on a place mat at a restaurant. Really? A restaurant? Well, now I don't know what to think.
jokes.setup: Lisa cites her source for Edison facts
jokes.punchline: Homer treats restaurant placemat as credible authority
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - restaurant placemats as scholarly sources. Shows his gullibility and misplaced respect for authority.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: According to this book, Thomas Edison was a great inventor who revolutionized the world with his discoveries.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's nothing. I learned that Edison invented the light bulb, the phonograph, AND spaghetti. It says so right here on this placemat at Krusty Burger.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, Edison didn't invent spaghetti.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, if it's on the placemat, it must be true. The placemat is a very reliable source.

jokes.text: They won't let me in the big people library downtown. There was some... unpleasantness. I can never go back.
jokes.setup: Homer explains why he's at elementary school library
jokes.punchline: Vague reference to being banned from main library
jokes.explanation: Great use of vague backstory. The undefined 'unpleasantness' is funnier than any specific incident would be.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, they banned me from the main library after the... incident with the beanbag chairs and the industrial lubricant.

jokes.text: And look at him dance. [vocalizing and laughing]
jokes.setup: Homer reading about Edison's inventions
jokes.punchline: Gets distracted by picture of Edison dancing
jokes.explanation: Homer's childlike fascination with visual elements over important facts. Character-appropriate distraction.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, Edison invented the light bulb, the phonograph, and the motion picture camera. What a guy!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, there's a picture of him dancing? D'oh!

jokes.text: No, not this one: The Smugglers of Pirate Cove. It's about pirates.
jokes.setup: Bart says all Hardy Boys books are about smugglers
jokes.punchline: Corrects himself with book obviously about pirates based on title
jokes.explanation: Simple joke about obvious book titles. Functional but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: All Hardy Boys books are about smugglers.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, except for 'The Pirates of Penzance' — that one's obviously about pirates.

jokes.text: I think it's pretty obvious that I am. Go school!
jokes.setup: Librarian asks if Homer is a student
jokes.punchline: Homer's awkward attempt to fit in with school spirit
jokes.explanation: Homer's terrible attempt to sound like a student. The generic 'Go school!' is perfectly wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Librarian
dialogue.line: Are you a student here?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh, yeah. Yeah, I'm a student. Go... Wildcats!

jokes.text: So, this broad stands up in the ocean... and this big wave knocks her bathing suit off
jokes.setup: Lenny starting what seems like an inappropriate story
jokes.punchline: Classic setup for crude humor
jokes.explanation: Setup for typical Lenny crude humor, predictable but in character

jokes.text: All I can think of now is Edison. I can't even remember where I work.
jokes.setup: Homer's Edison lecture interrupts Lenny's story
jokes.punchline: Homer's lecture was so boring it caused memory loss
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the boring lecture consequences. Memory loss is an absurd but logical outcome.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: So there I was, right? And this guy says to me—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Did you know that Thomas Edison invented the light bulb? And the phonograph? And he was afraid of the dark?

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Homer, I'm trying to tell a story here.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, sorry. Continue.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: So there I was, right? And this guy says to me... uh... what was I saying?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I don't know, but whatever it was, it was probably boring compared to Thomas Edison.

jokes.text: He was a shameless self-promoter. Well, you're not Thomas Edison.
jokes.setup: Homer defends Edison's self-promotion
jokes.punchline: Marge's cutting observation about Homer not being Edison
jokes.explanation: Marge's practical reality check deflates Homer's identification with Edison. Sharp and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, sure Edison electrocuted an elephant, but he also invented the light bulb. You gotta take the good with the bad.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you're not Thomas Edison.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Not yet, Marge. Not yet.

jokes.text: That's it! That's why I haven't done anything with my life. I need to be more like Thomas Edison.
jokes.setup: Marge points out Homer isn't Edison
jokes.punchline: Homer completely misses the point and decides to become Edison
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - taking the opposite lesson from what was intended. Great character consistency and misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you're not Edison.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You're right! I'll become Edison!

jokes.text: Do us a favor. Invent yourself some underpants.
jokes.setup: Homer declares himself an inventor while naked
jokes.punchline: Bart's practical suggestion for his first invention
jokes.explanation: Good comeback that addresses both Homer's inventor delusions and his current state of undress. Economical and practical.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, maybe your first invention should be pants.

jokes.text: You said I should quit my job and become an inventor, or you'd torch the house. That doesn't sound like me.
jokes.setup: Homer claims Marge told him to quit
jokes.punchline: Homer's obviously false version of conversation vs. Marge's reaction
jokes.explanation: Homer's selective memory and tendency to blame others. The arson threat addition is absurd but believable for his fabrication.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You told me to quit my job at the plant!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I never said that.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Then how do you explain this? You said, 'Homer, quit your job immediately. I insist. Here's a suitcase full of money to help you get started.'

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I said no such thing!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And then you said, 'I love you so much, Homer. You're the greatest man alive.'

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That is not what happened.

jokes.text: Not the way I quit
jokes.setup: Marge suggesting Homer could return to work
jokes.punchline: Implies Homer quit spectacularly badly
jokes.explanation: Mysterious reference to Homer's dramatic job exit

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, why don't you go back to work?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, I can't go back there. After I drove the nuclear power plant into the lake, they said I could never show my face there again.

jokes.text: Not the way I quit.
jokes.setup: Marge suggesting Homer could go back to the plant
jokes.punchline: Implied dramatic or offensive quitting method
jokes.explanation: Great implied backstory - suggests Homer burned bridges in spectacular fashion

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: You could always go back to the plant.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, I don't think Mr. Burns would take me back after the way I left things.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: What do you mean? How did you leave?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let's just say it involved a lot of fire and Mr. Burns' toupee.

jokes.text: You look at this table and what do you see? Just a table. Now a creative person like me looks at this table... and sees all kinds of creative things, but no tables.
jokes.setup: Homer explains creative vision to Marge
jokes.punchline: Pretentious explanation of seeing beyond the obvious
jokes.explanation: Good satire of pretentious creative types. Homer's pseudo-intellectual explanation is appropriately pompous.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You see, Marge, I have a creative vision. I don't just see what's there — I see what could be there. I see beyond the surface, into the very soul of things.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you're just looking at a donut.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly! A donut. But also... a metaphor for life itself.

jokes.text: Homer, that's not a table. That's our dryer. [Homer shrieks] My files!
jokes.setup: Homer's creative vision speech about table
jokes.punchline: It's actually the dryer running, destroying his papers
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup and payoff. Homer's pretentious speech is deflated by practical reality and consequences. Excellent structure.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You see, Marge, this table represents my creative vision. Every dent, every scratch tells a story of genius at work.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's actually the dryer running in the basement.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? No, no, I'm sure it's my creative genius.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Your papers are in there.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh my God! My life's work!

jokes.text: Yeah, he invented stuff too. - Shut up.
jokes.setup: Homer's smoking justification
jokes.punchline: Lisa's dry observation and Homer's defensive response
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa deadpan followed by Homer's childish defensiveness

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, smoking is one of the leading causes of death in this country.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, Lisa, Lisa. Smoking is also one of the leading causes of LIVING. If there were no smoking, there would be way too many people.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's not a good argument, Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, you're just saying that because you're a non-smoker!

jokes.text: Yes. Thomas Edison smoked several cigars a day. Yeah, he invented stuff too. Shut up.
jokes.setup: Bart asks if Homer started smoking
jokes.punchline: Homer's priorities: copying Edison's smoking but dismissing the inventing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - copying the unhealthy habits while dismissing the actual achievements. Good character insight.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you started smoking?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, Thomas Edison smoked cigars, so I figured if it was good enough for him...

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But Dad, Edison invented the light bulb!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, he also invented a lot of other stuff I'm not doing.

jokes.text: Automatic- Butt. Fluorescent- Booger.
jokes.setup: Homer asks kids for brainstorming session
jokes.punchline: Kids give him juvenile responses
jokes.explanation: Kid-appropriate sabotage of Homer's serious moment. Characters acting their age appropriately.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay kids, I need your help. I'm having a brainstorming session. I need ideas.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: How about 'Butt-scratching'?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I suggest we focus on constructive solutions.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Or 'Nose-picking Olympics'!

jokes.text: Both of you go to your rooms and spank yourselves. Lazy father. Can't even spank his own kids.
jokes.setup: Homer punishes the kids
jokes.punchline: Self-spanking punishment and kids' accurate assessment
jokes.explanation: Absurd punishment method followed by accurate character assessment. Kids seeing through Homer's laziness is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's it! You're both grounded! And as punishment, you have to spank yourselves!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, that doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Yes, it's logically inconsistent with basic behavioral psychology.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Just do it!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: He's really not thinking this through.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: No, he really isn't.

jokes.text: Lazy father. - Can't even spank his own kids
jokes.setup: Homer's self-spanking punishment
jokes.punchline: Kids correctly identifying Homer's laziness
jokes.explanation: Kids accurately critiquing Homer's parenting style

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you're supposed to spank yourself as punishment.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know, boy. But I got tired after two swats.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's the laziest punishment I've ever seen.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, Dad. You couldn't even commit to spanking yourself.

jokes.text: I don't see why not. They're my kids. I own 'em. - Okay. We own 'em
jokes.setup: Homer defending his arbitrary punishment
jokes.punchline: Treating kids as property, then correcting to include Marge
jokes.explanation: Homer's possessive view of children with quick correction for Marge

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Kids are property! Well, I mean, kids and wives are property.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? I'm trying to include you.

jokes.text: I guess because there's so much dolphin in it... and you know how smart they are.
jokes.setup: Marge says tuna is brain food
jokes.punchline: Confused reasoning about dolphin content and intelligence
jokes.explanation: Marge's rare moment of confused logic about dolphin-safe tuna. Subverts her usual practical nature.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Tuna is brain food.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, wait. Aren't dolphins smarter than humans?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wouldn't that make it... people food?

jokes.text: Microcalifragilistics. [Professor Frink babbles]
jokes.setup: Frink lists scientific fields
jokes.punchline: Made-up Mary Poppins reference field and characteristic babbling
jokes.explanation: Good character moment with pop culture reference. Frink's scientific gibberish mixed with Disney reference.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Well, there's ornithology, the study of birds; ichthyology, the study of fish; and of course, Poppinsology, the study of magical nannies who arrive on the East Wind with a mysterious carpet bag. Giggity-gootenploink!

jokes.text: You mean like an electric blanket-mobile?
jokes.setup: Frink explains invention process
jokes.punchline: Homer's immediate terrible invention idea
jokes.explanation: Homer's typical combination of two random things. Shows his shallow understanding of invention process.

jokes.text: Hamburger earmuffs!
jokes.setup: Frink suggests finding new uses for existing things
jokes.punchline: Another ridiculous Homer invention combining food and clothing
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer food-obsessed invention. Economical and character-appropriate absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Frink
dialogue.line: Well, gentlemen, sometimes the best inventions are finding new uses for existing things.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: New uses? Like this pants that are also a ham?

dialogue.speaker: Frink
dialogue.line: No, no, that's... that's not what I meant at all.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You can wear it, and if you get hungry, you just take a bite out of your own leg!

jokes.text: Thanks, sucker!
jokes.setup: Homer gets invention advice from Professor Frink
jokes.punchline: Immediately betrays the helpful professor
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer selfishness and lack of gratitude. Economical character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Well, Homer, if you want to invent something successful, you need to think about what people really need.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ah, good idea, Professor! Thanks for all your help!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm going to go patent your idea and sell it myself!

jokes.text: These babies will be in the stores... while he's still grappling with the pickle matrix.
jokes.setup: Homer thinks he's outsmarted Frink
jokes.punchline: Nonsensical technical phrase 'pickle matrix'
jokes.explanation: Homer using pseudo-technical language he doesn't understand. 'Pickle matrix' is perfectly nonsensical.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I outsmarted Frink with my pickle matrix!

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: A pickle matrix? That's not even a real scientific concept!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, it fooled you, didn't it?

jokes.text: All-purpose electric hammer demonstration disaster
jokes.setup: Homer demonstrating his electric hammer
jokes.punchline: Tool clearly dangerous and impractical
jokes.explanation: Standard slapstick showing Homer's invention incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Behold! My electric hammer! It combines the power of electricity with the usefulness of a hammer.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Homer, that thing looks dangerous.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dangerous? Pfft. What could possibly go wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Yeah, what could go wrong with an electric hammer?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly! It's perfectly safe and practical!

jokes.text: [Electric hammer shocking Homer] Probably needs to be more powerful.
jokes.setup: Homer demonstrates electric hammer
jokes.punchline: Device hurts him but he wants more power
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer missing the obvious problem with his invention. Physical comedy with character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ow! Ow! Ow! Wait, wait, wait. Can you turn it up?

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Homer, are you sure?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, I'm sure. More power!

jokes.text: Probably needs to be more powerful.
jokes.setup: Electric hammer causing chaos and injury
jokes.punchline: Homer's understated assessment of the obvious problem
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan response to obvious disaster - Homer learning the wrong lesson

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I guess the electric hammer isn't working out.

jokes.text: I'll buy 10 of those right now.
jokes.setup: Homer's dangerous electric hammer demonstration
jokes.punchline: Marge's sarcastic enthusiasm for the terrible product
jokes.explanation: Good sarcastic response that shows Marge's patience wearing thin. Economical and well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh Homer, an electric hammer! What could possibly go wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I'm sure this will be the safest, most practical tool anyone has ever invented.

jokes.text: This will sound every three seconds... unless something isn't okay.
jokes.setup: Homer presents 'Everything's Okay' alarm
jokes.punchline: Backwards logic - alarm sounds when things ARE okay
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reversal of alarm logic. The backwards utility makes it perfectly useless and annoying.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I've invented the 'Everything's Okay' alarm. It sounds when everything IS okay.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: That doesn't make any sense, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly! That's why it's brilliant!

jokes.text: It can't be turned off. But it, uh, does break easily.
jokes.setup: Alarm is driving everyone crazy
jokes.punchline: Homer's admission of poor construction as if it's a feature
jokes.explanation: Great recovery line that presents a fatal flaw as a selling point. Perfect Homer rationalization.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The alarm goes off every morning at 5 AM, and I can't figure out how to turn it off.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you've had that alarm clock for three years.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, that's the beauty of it! The manufacturers designed it so you can't turn it off. It builds character!

jokes.text: It's for the woman who only has four-fifths of a second to get ready.
jokes.setup: Makeup gun introduction
jokes.punchline: Ridiculously specific and short time frame
jokes.explanation: Absurdist specificity - the oddly precise time measurement for getting ready

jokes.text: It's for the woman who only has four-fifths of a second to get ready.
jokes.setup: Homer presents makeup gun for busy women
jokes.punchline: Ridiculously specific and impossible time frame
jokes.explanation: Absurd precision in the time measurement makes the concept even more ridiculous. Good specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's a makeup gun for women who have zero time!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You just aim it at your face and fire! One Mississippi...

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's dangerous!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh Marge, I've done a lot of crazy things. Remember the time I ate all the donuts?

jokes.text: Homer, you've got it set on 'whore.'
jokes.setup: Homer applies makeup gun to Marge
jokes.punchline: Reveals terrible makeup application with blunt assessment
jokes.explanation: Perfect punchline that's both visual gag and dialogue. Marge's matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, what do you think? I'm pretty good with a gun.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my God!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You look like a clown.

jokes.text: Now I have to go get my cold cream gun
jokes.setup: Makeup gun disaster
jokes.punchline: Implies Homer has made multiple beauty-related weapons
jokes.explanation: Good escalation implying Homer's pattern of weaponizing beauty products

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer! What have you done?!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I was just trying to help! I invented a makeup gun!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: A makeup gun? Homer, that's the third beauty weapon you've made this month!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, you said you wanted to get ready faster...

jokes.text: Women will like what I tell 'em to like.
jokes.setup: Bart says women won't like being shot in the face
jokes.punchline: Homer's misogynistic confidence
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate chauvinism that's meant to be obviously wrong. Dark humor that shows Homer's flaws.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Women won't like being shot in the face.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Not with that attitude.

jokes.text: But now, with the Lazy Man Reclining Toilet Chair... you can just lean back and let 'er rip.
jokes.setup: Homer describes old TV watching difficulties
jokes.punchline: Toilet chair solution with crude expression
jokes.explanation: Peak lazy invention that perfectly represents Homer's values. The crude phrasing is character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Back then, if you wanted to watch TV, you had to sit in an uncomfortable chair for hours.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So I got myself a toilet chair. Now I can watch TV and crap at the same time!

jokes.text: Sure. Believe me, every man in America will want to have one.
jokes.setup: Marge questions toilet chair concept
jokes.punchline: Homer's deluded confidence in universal male laziness
jokes.explanation: Homer projecting his laziness onto all men. Character-specific delusion about his terrible invention.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, what is this? A toilet chair?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's a chair you can sit on while you sit on the toilet. Every man will want one.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, no man wants to sit on a toilet chair.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You're just not thinking like a man, Marge. We're very lazy.

jokes.text: Gangway! Gotta poop. No, Bart!
jokes.setup: Homer demonstrates toilet chair
jokes.punchline: Bart immediately wants to use it inappropriately
jokes.explanation: Bart's immediate practical application of Homer's invention. Kid-appropriate gross-out humor with good timing.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Behold! The toilet chair. You can sit and eat at the same time.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Cool! Can I use it to sit and play video games?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, Bart, it's for eating.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What about sitting and watching TV?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Sitting and reading magazines?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Absolutely not!

jokes.text: I'm not saying you're a bad inventor. I'm just saying these particular... inventions are awful... and no one in their right mind would buy them or accept them as gifts.
jokes.setup: Marge tries to be diplomatic about Homer's inventions
jokes.punchline: Escalating criticism that ends with brutal honesty
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation from diplomatic to devastating. Marge's attempt at kindness makes the final blow harder.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, Homer, that's... certainly creative.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You like it?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: It's just that, well, it's not very practical.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Not practical? Marge, it's a masterpiece!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Honey, it's on fire.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's the best part!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you're a moron. A complete and utter moron.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: I guess I'm no better at being Thomas Edison than I was at being Homer Simpson.
jokes.setup: Homer realizes his inventions are terrible
jokes.punchline: Self-aware comment about failing at being himself
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke about character identity. Homer acknowledging he's bad at being himself is perfectly self-aware.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not good at inventing things. I'm not even good at being Homer Simpson.

jokes.text: I guess I'll just give up my hopes and dreams... and settle for being a decent husband and father.
jokes.setup: Homer decides to give up inventing
jokes.punchline: Treating basic family responsibility as 'settling'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer perspective - seeing basic decency as a consolation prize. Good character insight.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's it! I'm done inventing. From now on, I'm just going to be a regular father and husband.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's wonderful! You're finally taking responsibility for your family.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I guess I'm settling.

jokes.text: I stuck a couple extra legs on there... 'cause I kept tipping over when I was trying to invent stuff. They're on hinges.
jokes.setup: Family worried Homer fell
jokes.punchline: Homer's accidental practical invention from personal clumsiness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - accidentally inventing something useful while failing at everything else. Great irony.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh no! Homer's fallen off the roof!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: We have to call an ambulance!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, Dad's okay. And he invented something.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's a... uh... safety ladder that deploys when you fall. I tripped over the gutters and it just happened!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Simpson, this is genius! I'll buy the patent for three dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo!

jokes.text: Oh, yeah. Safety sells, especially to lame-o's. I'd buy one. Me too.
jokes.setup: Family evaluates Homer's chair invention
jokes.punchline: Bart's market analysis and family's genuine interest
jokes.explanation: Bart's cynical but accurate business insight. The family's genuine interest validates Homer's first good idea.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, this chair is actually pretty sweet. I'd say it's got real market potential. Could sell maybe 30, 40 units tops.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The ergonomic design is quite sound, Father. I believe Bart's estimate may be conservative.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, we really do think this is wonderful. We're not just saying that.

jokes.text: Oh, damn it!
jokes.setup: Homer discovers Edison already invented his chair
jokes.punchline: Crushing realization ruins his triumph
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Homer's brief success. Simple but effective emotional beat.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, Edison invented the electric chair? But I thought I invented it!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh! All my hard work... wasted.

jokes.text: Heard you swearin'. Mind if I join in? Crap, boobs, crap!
jokes.setup: Bart hears Homer curse
jokes.punchline: Kid-appropriate 'bad words' with enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart opportunism to join in bad behavior. Kid-appropriate rebellion with good energy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wow, Dad! You said a bad word!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I did?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah! You said 'D'oh!' That's so cool!

jokes.text: So, we've got to go to the Edison Museum and smash it! Then I'll be an inventor.
jokes.setup: Homer realizes Edison beat him to the invention
jokes.punchline: Solution is to destroy historical evidence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - if you can't beat history, destroy it. Escalation to criminal activity is character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, Edison invented the light bulb before me? That's not fair!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know! I'll just destroy all the historical evidence that he did it first.

jokes.text: So, we've got to go to the Edison Museum and smash it! Then I'll be an inventor
jokes.setup: Homer discovering Edison's similar chair
jokes.punchline: Plan to destroy evidence rather than invent something new
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - destroy evidence rather than create something original

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. This is just like my idea!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm gonna destroy this evidence and tell everyone I invented it first.

jokes.text: Ah, the hell with him. Yeah! Hell, damn, fart!
jokes.setup: Homer turns against Edison
jokes.punchline: Bart joining in with more kid swearing
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier swearing with escalation. Bart and Homer bonding over rebellion.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's it! I'm done with Edison! He's a fraud and a charlatan!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, man! He sucks!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Damn right he does!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: This is ass!

jokes.text: Oh, and later he raised turkeys. That was on his turkey farm.
jokes.setup: Homer listing Edison Jr.'s failed ventures
jokes.punchline: Obvious statement about where turkey farming happens
jokes.explanation: Homer stating the obvious as if it's insightful information. Simple but character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Edison Jr.'s failed ventures include a turkey farm.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, of course turkeys are raised on farms.

jokes.text: By smashing my chair, you're only hurting yourself.
jokes.setup: Homer about to smash Edison's chair
jokes.punchline: Edison's ghost appears with philosophical comment
jokes.explanation: Supernatural intervention with self-help philosophy. Good escalation of the museum plot.

dialogue.speaker: Edison's Ghost
dialogue.line: You know, I invented the electric chair. I didn't think it would be used this way.

jokes.text: Stop, Homer! By smashing my chair, you're only hurting yourself
jokes.setup: Homer about to smash Edison's chair
jokes.punchline: Edison's ghost appears with pseudo-wisdom
jokes.explanation: Supernatural intervention with typical self-help clichés

dialogue.speaker: Edison's ghost
dialogue.line: Wait! Before you destroy that chair, consider this: a man who destroys the furniture of great inventors is himself no inventor.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. That's pretty deep.

dialogue.speaker: Edison's ghost
dialogue.line: I know. I've had a lot of time to think about it.

jokes.text: I'll get you, you fat lunatic!
jokes.setup: Homer escapes from museum guard
jokes.punchline: Guard's frustrated insult and chase
jokes.explanation: Standard chase scene with blunt insult. Functional but not particularly creative.

dialogue.speaker: Security Guard
dialogue.line: Why you little...

dialogue.speaker: Security Guard
dialogue.line: Get back here!

jokes.text: Just one: Thomas Edison. That's very good. And that's true too. It's funny and true.
jokes.setup: Guide asks riddle about lightbulb invention
jokes.punchline: Self-congratulatory analysis of his own joke
jokes.explanation: Meta-commentary on joke quality. Character's self-assessment is appropriately awkward for tour guide.

dialogue.speaker: Museum Guide
dialogue.line: Now, who can tell me who invented the lightbulb?

dialogue.speaker: Museum Guide
dialogue.line: That's right, Thomas Edison. And do you know what Thomas Edison said after he invented the lightbulb?

dialogue.speaker: Museum Guide
dialogue.line: "Let there be light!" Hehehehe. I crack myself up.

jokes.text: How many geniuses does it take to invent a lightbulb? Just one: Thomas Edison
jokes.setup: Tour guide asking riddle
jokes.punchline: Anti-joke with straightforward answer
jokes.explanation: Anti-joke format subverting expected punchline structure

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: What has four wheels and flies?

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: A garbage truck!

jokes.text: Invent your way out of this, Edison!
jokes.setup: Homer attacks Edison's chair with hammer
jokes.punchline: Taunting a dead inventor while vandalizing museum
jokes.explanation: Homer's petty rivalry with historical figure. Good character moment of misplaced aggression.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Take that, Edison! And that! Your genius can't help you now!

jokes.text: You mean the wild mood swings?
jokes.setup: Homer says he's like Edison
jokes.punchline: Bart focuses on Homer's mental instability
jokes.explanation: Bart's astute observation about Homer's psychological state. Good character insight with economy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm like Edison. I'm a genius.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, except Edison actually invented something.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? What about all my great ideas?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, the only thing you've invented is new ways to be a total doofus.

jokes.text: Uh, I think that's in Italy, Dad. Oh. Well, then we'll take it out on Eli Whitney.
jokes.setup: Homer suggests da Vinci museum next
jokes.punchline: Settles for more accessible historical figure to vandalize
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer practicality in vandalism planning. Eli Whitney as backup target is random and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: How about we go to the da Vinci museum?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Nah, let's find something more accessible to vandalize.

jokes.text: Authorities say the phony pope can be recognized by his high-top sneakers and incredibly foul mouth
jokes.setup: News report about fake pope
jokes.punchline: Specific and absurd identifying characteristics
jokes.explanation: Absurd news story with specific funny details

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: We interrupt this program for a special news bulletin. The Pope has arrived in Springfield. Repeat, the Pope is here.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: He's a man of God who wears a tall hat and funny shoes.

jokes.text: That was your idea. This brilliant innovation is expected to generate millions... for Edison's already-wealthy heirs.
jokes.setup: News reports Edison's 'new' inventions
jokes.punchline: Homer's ideas credited to Edison, making rich family richer
jokes.explanation: Final irony of episode - Homer's good ideas attributed to Edison. Good closure to main theme.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: We interrupt this program to bring you a special report. Thomas Edison has done it again with yet another amazing invention.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: In a stunning turn of events, what we thought were Homer Simpson's original ideas have been credited to the great inventor Thomas Edison.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: This has made the already wealthy Edison family even richer, while Homer receives nothing.

jokes.text: Let's just say I'm sitting in the right chair.
jokes.setup: Family asks how Homer's taking the news
jokes.punchline: Pun about his safety chair being the real success
jokes.explanation: Good closing pun that references his actual successful invention. Economical ending with wordplay.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, how are you taking the news?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I'm sitting in my safety chair, so I'd say I'm taking it pretty well.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that's not what we meant.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know, but this chair is the real success story here.

tmdb_overview: A midlife crisis prompts Homer to quit his job and become an inventor.
insight: Homer's garage-fire obsession drives 81 jokes in 22 minutes—absurdist excess over satirical bite.
